title: Functions, deeper
description: Functions as values, callbacks, arrow functions, and closures.
objectives: Treat functions as values you can store, pass, and return
Read and write arrow function syntax fluently
Pass a function to another function (callback)
Use a closure to remember a value across calls
recap: You can now read code that passes functions around — and it stops looking like magic. The main task taught the callback shape: a function takes another function, runs it, gets a value back. The practice exercises went one floor deeper into closures — a returned function that *remembers* a value from where it was made. With these in hand, the array methods in the next lesson (`map`, `filter`) will read like English instead of mystery.
practicePrompts: Write a function that takes another function and a number, and returns whichever is bigger: the result of applying the function to the number, or the number itself.
Make a function `delay(fn)` that doesn't run `fn` immediately, but returns a function that will run it when called.

practiceExercises.title: Make an adder
practiceExercises.prompt: Write a function `makeAdder(n)` that returns a NEW function. The new function takes one argument and returns it plus `n`. For example, `const add5 = makeAdder(5)` gives you a function such that `add5(3)` returns `8` and `add5(100)` returns `105`. The same `n` is reused across every call to the returned function — that's a closure.
practiceExercises.goal: See a closure in action: a returned function that remembers a value from where it was made.
hints: Inside `makeAdder`, can you return a function that uses `n`?
`return (x) => x + n;` — the returned function takes `x` and adds the captured `n`.
When the returned function runs later, JavaScript still remembers what `n` was when `makeAdder` was called. That memory is the closure.

practiceExercises.title: Compose two functions
practiceExercises.prompt: Write a function `compose(f, g)` that returns a NEW function. The new function takes one argument `x` and returns the result of running `g` first, then passing that result through `f`. For example, with `addOne = n => n + 1` and `double = n => n * 2`, then `compose(double, addOne)(5)` should return `12` (5 + 1 = 6, 6 * 2 = 12).
practiceExercises.goal: Return a function that uses both `f` and `g` from the outer call.
hints: You need to return a function. Think about what that returned function does when it's called later — it has access to both `f` and `g`.
Inside the returned function, you'll call `g` on the input first, and then pass that result to `f`.

practiceExercises.title: Run-it-only-once wrapper
practiceExercises.prompt: Write a function `once(fn)` that returns a NEW function. The new function calls `fn(...)` the first time it's invoked and returns the result. On every subsequent call it should NOT run `fn` again — it should return the same first result. Useful for things you only ever want to do one time (like an initial setup).
practiceExercises.goal: Use a closure to remember whether something has happened, plus its result.

tests.name: second call returns same value, fn did not re-run
tests.call: (() => { let n = 0; const f = once(() => { n = n + 1; return n; }); f(); f(); return n; })()

tests.name: third call still cached
tests.call: (() => { let n = 0; const f = once(() => { n = n + 1; return n; }); f(); f(); f(); return f(); })()

tests.name: two wrappers are independent
tests.call: (() => { const a = once(() => "A"); const b = once(() => "B"); return a() + b() + a() + b(); })()

hints: You need TWO things to remember between calls: a boolean (`hasRun`) and the cached return value (`result`). Declare both inside `once`, before you return the wrapped function.
Inside the wrapped function: if `hasRun` is true, return `result`. Otherwise call `fn()`, store the answer in `result`, set `hasRun = true`, and return.
`let hasRun = false; let result; return () => { if (!hasRun) { result = fn(); hasRun = true; } return result; };`
